data.title: Cooper-Standard Holdings: When Price Isn't The Only Thing That Matters
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I have an appreciation for the automotive sector. Much of the modern economy was built around the automobile. And anything that makes this economy move is of interest to me. It's also a sector that, even though it's prone to disruption, will only continue to grow from here on out. Or at least it will, absent some major change in technology or demographics.

One company in this space that is worth knowing about is Cooper-Standard Holdings (CPS). But there is a difference between worth knowing about it and it being an attractive prospect. In some ways, the company is appealing. For instance, shares are trading on the cheap. On the other hand, financial performance has been volatile, and sales as of late have been declining. When you add on top of this the fact that economic conditions are worsening, and that automotive sales are likely to be lower this year than they were last year, I believe that the most generous rating that I can assign the company is a "Hold."

Taking Cooper-Standard Holdings for a drive

The management team at Cooper-Standard Holdings describes the company as a producer of sealing and fluid handling systems. Examples here include fuel and brake delivery systems and fluid transfer systems. These products are mostly used in passenger vehicles and light trucks. Overwhelmingly, the company caters to the OEM space, with 86% of its revenue in 2024 coming from players there such as Ford Motor Company (F), General Motors (GM), and others. And while the company does have a significant presence in the United States, it does have some foreign operations as well. Specifically, I'm talking about China, where some of its major customers include BYD, Geely, and Chery.

The company does generate some revenue outside of the OEM space. In fact, 14% of sales came from automotive suppliers, non-automotive customers, and replacement part distributors. Given the fact that individuals are keeping their vehicles longer than ever before, which should increase demand for aftermarket parts, I would actually prefer that the business operate as an enterprise centered around the aftermarket industry. But on the whole, this does not invalidate it as an investment opportunity.

In recent years, Cooper-Standard Holdings has exhibited mixed financial performance. Revenue increased from $2.53 billion in 2022 to $2.82 billion in 2023. But then, in 2024, revenue dropped to $2.73 billion. The company's revenue is highly correlated to the light vehicle production market. And I don't just mean that for the United States. I mean it globally as well. In 2023, North American light vehicle production amounted to 15.7 million units. In 2024, that number dropped to 15.5 million. Most other markets saw increases. But not all of them. Europe, for instance, saw a decline from 18 million vehicles to 17.1 million. At the time that management announced results for the 2024 fiscal year as a whole, they expected declining sales in both North America and Europe for 2025. I don't have any reliable data regarding Europe. But I do know that, in the United States, we actually saw an increase to 16.2 million new vehicle sales. This far exceeded the 15.1 million that management was anticipating for North America.

Unfortunately, as the first chart in this article illustrates, bottom line performance for the business has been all over the map. Seeing as how its customers are largely big players in the automotive space, exercising significant influence over their own supply chains, and considering that companies such as Cooper-Standard Holdings typically have to abide by longer-term contracts, and that we went through a window of inflationary pain, it's not unreasonable to see mixed financial performance. More important than what happened over the last couple of years, however, is what happened last year.

During the first nine months of 2025, revenue for the company was virtually flat compared to the same time a year earlier. It amounted to about $2.07 billion. This seems to have been driven by a couple of things, including foreign currency fluctuations and increases in volume and product mix. We do know that, in China, light vehicle production jumped 11.7% year over year, from 20.6 million vehicles to 23 million. But outside of China and South America, there was some weakness. Even though new vehicle sales in the United States ended up being higher in 2025, actual production numbers dropped during the first nine months to 11.7 million from the 11.8 million that we saw the previous year. In Europe, production declined from 12.8 million to 12.6 million.

Bottom line results are worthy of our attention. The company went from a net loss of $119 million to a net loss of only $7.5 million. A reduction in restructuring charges of $11.9 million played a role here. But the bigger contributor was the fact that, during the first nine months of 2024, the company was hit by a $44.6 million pension settlement charge that did not repeat in 2025. Other profitability metrics ended up being positive, surprisingly. Operating cash flow jumped from $1.6 million to $8.2 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $28.6 million to $83.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $136.7 million to $180.5 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management said that investors should expect revenue of between $2.68 billion and $2.72 billion. Even at the high end, that would unfortunately represent a decline from the $2.73 billion reported for 2024. This would be the second consecutive year of revenue declines. Meanwhile, we should see some improvement in profitability, with EBITDA rising from $180.7 million in 2024 to between $200 million and $210 million in 2025. This would likely translate to an adjusted operating cash flow of about $49.3 million.

I would like to point out that these recent improvements that the company has captured have come from painstaking cost-cutting initiatives. Management has spent years rationalizing their manufacturing footprint by closing over a dozen manufacturing facilities and selling off some of its non-performing operations. It has centralized equipment specification and sourcing decisions, and has even reduced headcount. The company has also been open with investors about renegotiating supplier contracts. This is especially important in the era of tariffs. From 2019 through the third quarter of 2025, the company claims to have captured cumulative cost savings of about $715 million on an annual basis. A whopping $85 million of that came from the end of 2024 through the third quarter of 2025. So it is likely that some improvements will continue moving forward.

This is great in and of itself. However, that's not the only thing that should go into whether this makes for a good prospect. One item is valuation. Using the aforementioned estimates for 2025, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. In that same chart, I compared the company to five other similar firms. In addition to looking decently priced on an absolute basis, the firm is deeper than all but two of these competitors when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. But it’s cheaper than only one of the five when it comes to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach.

The long-term picture for the company will likely be positive. According to management, these are rather sizable markets that it plays in. For instance, the global sealing systems market, which the company has a 16% stake in, is worth about $8.5 billion. Even larger is the fluid handling systems market. Management claims that the company is the second largest player on the planet here, with an 8.3% market share. However, this is a massive market, worth $14.4 billion in total. Management is trying to capture more of these markets by making investments in other opportunities. For instance, by focusing on newer innovative products, the company achieved $228.5 million of net new business awards during the first nine months of 2025. 87% of these related to innovative products. 83% involved battery electric or hybrid vehicle programs. And an impressive 62% focused on high growth Chinese OEMs.

Normally, these things would cause me to be at least a bit bullish. Having said that, I am concerned about where the economy is more broadly. As I have detailed in other articles, the United States seems to be heading toward a recession. China, as I detailed in another article, is also seeing its growth slow. Considering that, in the most recent quarter, 58.9% of the company's revenue came from North America while another 14.2% came from the Asia Pacific region, what goes on in both the United States and China is relevant to the business. We do know that, when it comes to the United States specifically, Cox Automotive forecasted that new vehicle sales this year will be about 15.8 million units. If this comes to fruition, it would be down from 2.4% compared to what the industry saw last year. Worries about inflation, tariffs, and disruptive effects caused by front-loading of purchases last year to get ahead of tariffs and the expiration of the $7,500 electric vehicle tax credit, certainly are playing roles here. And honestly, given my own view about the economy, I suspect that economic pain will be worse here than many analysts anticipate.

Takeaway

Long term, I would argue that Cooper-Standard Holdings is doing a decent job. However, shares are not cheap enough, especially in light of my own concerns about the economy, to justify a terribly bullish outlook. At the end of the day, I believe that this combination of factors justifies a "Hold" rating more than it does anything else.

data.title: Prologis: Entering A Phase Of Accelerated Earnings Growth
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Prologis (PLD) because the business is entering a much more favorable phase of the cycle with multiple tailwinds lining up at the same time. Core logistics fundamentals are inflecting as vacancy comes down and pricing power starts to return. On top of that, PLD’s exposure to energy and data centers adds a new growth element. Combined with a strong balance sheet, I see a setup where earnings growth can reaccelerate.

Company overview

PLD owns, manages, and develops logistics real estate. It has ~1.3 billion square feet across 19 countries, and the strategy is to focus on land-constrained, high-demand markets near major cities like Los Angeles, Tokyo, and London. In the past, PLD mainly leased logistics facilities to customers, primarily in business-to-business [B2B] and retail/online fulfillment categories (think e-commerce). Today, the focus tilts toward expanding into digital infrastructure and energy solutions (think data centers and renewable energy).

Q4 2025 Earnings Review

Q4 2025 was an important quarter that made me turn bullish for PLD. Rental revenue performance was solid, coming in at $2.09 billion, up from $1.94 billion last year. The quality of this growth is what matters because it was driven by lease mark-to-market, which should’ve been a headwind given the softening rates. Despite that, PLD continues to roll older leases onto higher rates. For perspective, PLD’s portfolio mark-to-market is ~18%, and what that means is that this 18% will eventually show up in the revenue over time (i.e., 18% growth is almost guaranteed even if other parts of the portfolio stay flat). Topline performance translated nicely into core FFO (funds from operations), which came in at $1.42/share (or FY2025 core FFO of $5.86). For 2026, management is guiding for core FFO of $6.15 (the midpoint), which implies 4.9% y/y growth. I believe this guide is too conservative considering the solid tailwinds (discussed below), and there is room for upside as the year progresses.

Key bull case points

I believe the setup for PLD today is the strongest in recent history. There are two points that matter here; the first is the real estate cycle. For most of recent history, the logistics real estate industry sentiment had been weighed down by rising vacancy, but that seems to be ending soon. In Q4 2025, PLD saw net absorption across its US markets up by 26% sequentially to 59 million square feet, and more importantly, absorption exceeded new supply deliveries for the first time since 2022. This is also in line with Cushman & Wakefield findings that there is renewal demand momentum. My view is simple here, grounded by basic economic knowledge. When demand exceeds supply, vacancy has to go down (which indeed, was down 10 basis points to 7.4% in Q4 for PLD).

Combined with the fact that new deliveries are set to slow meaningfully, with industry supply expected to fall from ~200 million square feet in 2025 to 180 million square feet in 2026 (data from Q4 2025 earnings transcript), it creates a demand/supply imbalance dynamic, which means pricing bargaining power should shift back to PLD’s hands. This sets up a backdrop where PLD’s rent growth could surprise the market on the upside.

The second bullish point is the energy and data center opportunity. The AI infrastructure investment supercycle is driving up demand across the entire value chain, from semiconductor chips to energy generation and also to data centers. Every part of the value chain is important, but access to power is a major bottleneck today. You can have all the chips and customers ready, but without access to power, the data centers cannot start. In this sense, PLD is a direct beneficiary because it has expanded its power pipeline to 5.7 GW (either secured or in advanced stages of development). Management has given a sense of the magnitude of opportunity here, guiding for data centers to potentially represent ~40% of development starts in 2026. That is a sizeable mix, and it should come with much more attractive yields since hyperscalers value time-to-market more than cost.

So long as the world demand for AI continues to be strong, which I think it will, this opportunity set is almost “guaranteed” as well, especially since data centers used for inferencing have to be in proximity to end users. As I said above, PLD already controls the land near major cities, so it is in a very good position to capitalize on this tailwind. Over time, as the portfolio mix becomes more data center related, we should see a positive impact on valuation as well as the market shifts its valuation paradigm from PLD being a pure-play logistics REIT to one that has exposure to the high-growth data center market.

Balance sheet

Of course, one cannot discuss a REIT without touching on the balance sheet. Ultimately, this is a business that needs to invest a lot in CAPEX to create the underlying assets to drive earnings growth. A good thing for PLD is that it sits on ~$7.6 billion in liquidity with a weighted average debt cost of just 3.3% (this is less than the US 1-year treasury note, for comparison). What this implies is that PLD has a structural cost advantage in today’s environment. Because its cost of capital is lower, it is able to price more aggressively vs. smaller developers. The large amount of liquidity that it has also means that PLD can fund and ramp up the development of data centers very quickly, putting it in a more favorable position vs. sub-scale players that lack funding.

Valuation

The primary assumption I have in my model is that FFO/share growth will accelerate back to high single digits by FY28, anchored by the two major tailwinds I mentioned earlier. This shouldn’t be a high bar since PLD has achieved this historically even without the AI tailwinds today. Without assuming any changes to the multiple, at 20x forward P/FFO multiple, the share price should be worth ~$142 by FY27. Add on the two years of dividends (~3.2% yield), and I can bridge to a total return of 18% over two years.

Investment Risk

Some investors may still look at PLD as a dividend REIT play, and that makes it highly sensitive to interest rate volatility. If rates go up for whatever reasons, the dividend yield spread may become much less attractive, and that could weigh on the share price. The pivot to data center development is great but also carries risk. If development yields are delayed by power permitting issues, this could become a drag on near-term margins.

Conclusion

My positive view on PLD is because of the cyclical recovery and structural growth tailwinds. The logistics portfolio is benefiting from tightening supply and improving demand, while the data center and energy pipeline add a layer of structural growth to the equity story. With a low cost of capital and ample liquidity, PLD can invest through the cycle and capitalize on the current trends.

data.title: European Natural Gas Prices Set To Surge: 2 Energy Stocks Are Particularly Well-Positioned
data.description:
Investment thesis

European natural gas inventories crossed below the five-year average at the start of 2025, and as of now, the gap widened to over 20% below the five-year average. It is a gap that amounts to about 17 billion cubic meters, or more or less equivalent to the annual demand in a country like Belgium or Poland. It is an indication of a substantial supply/demand gap based on available supplies at current market prices. In other words, Europe needs to bid the price up much higher if it is to attract more supplies to close that gap.

I started buying Equinor (EQNR) stock at the start of last year, in response to the widening supply/demand gap that I followed starting in 2024, when the trend of supply shortfalls became evident in the data. As the inventory situation gradually worsened, with no market reaction to the data, I continued buying incrementally throughout 2025, making Equinor my third-largest position in my portfolio.

I bought Cheniere (LNG) shares with the same external factor in mind. However, I only considered this investment after a roughly 20% decline in its share price from its all-time high. In other words, it was a combination of a significant company-specific dip in its stock, coupled with an intensifying positive external situation, that the market failed to react to for over a year, as it developed.

Now that the market is finally starting to notice the European natural gas fundamentals, with the natural gas spot price moving higher and likely to persist in this direction going forward, I want to evaluate the dynamics of each investment choice to figure out which one is more likely to outperform. While both companies are deeply tied to the European natural gas market, their company profiles differ substantially. Additional factors and possible outcomes can determine which stock will outperform.

Maintaining a buy rating for both Equinor and Cheniere

I last covered Equinor a month ago, when the European natural gas market was inexplicably falling, even as storage levels continued to dwindle at a fast pace. Its share price is currently up about 6% since that article. I continue to see it as a buy, given that I foresee higher European natural gas prices for the foreseeable future, as low inventory levels will push European buyers to bid up the price for the rest of the year to make up for the current inventory shortfall.

Cheniere became a buy in my books recently after the sizable selloff in its share price, which I highlighted in my December article. Its share price is up about 8% since then, and it remains a buy on the same assumption of positive external trends, as is the case with Equinor.

An inexplicable year on European natural gas markets

As I watched European natural gas inventories slide from being far above the five-year average in the fall of 2024 to crossing that level, dipping below by the end of that year, I expected European natural gas prices to react.

At first, there was a minor market reaction. Then European natural gas prices inexplicably fell throughout most of last year, even as inventories steadily declined relative to the five-year average.

The most baffling defiance of market fundamentals by the market occurred at the start of this year, as inventory levels have been firmly stuck at levels that are the second lowest in the past six years.

If inventories continue along the current path, it is projected that by March, we will be below the technically dangerous 1,000 bcf level, where some countries will have to start rationing gas.

As we can see, not only are we headed for a deep dive in inventory levels, below the 1,000 Bcf threshold, where a crisis may start to emerge at a regional level, but we are also projected to see Europe entering the next heating season at barely over half-full inventory levels, which would put it on course for a deep energy crisis by the start of the 2026-2027 winter.

Recent events may compound Europe's crisis

There has been a significant upswing in the US Henry Hub spot price for natural gas.

The price spike is mostly weather-related, which may be transitory. If the upswing continues or if prices persist at current levels, there is a good chance that the Trump administration will opt to intervene by reducing US LNG exports.

US natural gas imports currently make up between a fifth and a quarter of all European imports. At the same time, most of the US exports currently go to Europe. If the US were to reduce exports even by a modest 10%, by the fall, it would have a negative impact of about 6 Bcm or about 190 Bcf, meaning that inventories might not even reach 50% full in September, unless other sources become available or if there is another downturn in European gas demand.

The recent news that Japan's largest nuclear power plant had to suspend operations after restarting it just recently may mean that Japan will have to import higher-than-expected volumes of LNG this year. The potential for unforeseen supply disruptions, such as a Ukrainian attack on the Turkstream pipeline, which is the last pipeline route that carries Russian gas to Europe, or conflicts affecting countries like Azerbaijan or Algeria, are also a real risk. A slight pickup in US natural gas demand or a slight decline in output might have an outsized impact on European supplies. These are all potential wildcard factors that may harm Europe's already precarious natural gas storage level situation this year.

Betting on a return of Russian gas

One possible explanation for why the market took so long to react to the worsening European natural gas situation is that there was some hope that a resolution to the Ukraine war would allow at least a limited return of Russian pipeline gas supplies to Europe. If this is what the markets have been betting on, it would provide a somewhat rational explanation for the lack of reaction to the trend for the past year. I personally doubt that this is the explanation, which is in some ways worrying, because in the absence of the rational, what is left is the irrational, or worse, some sort of public-private agreement that prompted companies to ignore the worsening storage level situation, until recently, when it can no longer be ignored and bidding on extra supplies at ever higher prices needs to take place.

The partial return of the Russian gas supply angle is, in my view, an unrealistic market expectation for this year, if anyone is inclined to trade based on this assumption. Even if there is a peace deal between Ukraine & Russia, it would be very hard for Europe to unwind the rhetoric that was employed to support the continued support of Ukraine in the war, which came with high direct and indirect fiscal & economic costs. The Russian military threat to the entire European continent was framed as being a near-certain existential threat to all European nations. Pivoting from that to the Russians riding to the rescue of Europe's economy through a resumption of gas deliveries during a time of crisis would be a very tough sell, prompting obvious questions about the prior rhetoric and its economic consequences.

We should also keep in mind that the Russians greatly diversified their energy export markets since the Ukraine crisis started in 2014. Exports of Russian natural gas to China rose in December to a new record high of 5.8 Bcm. On an annualized rate, that volume is equivalent to about half of all the gas that was delivered to the EU before the war. In other words, the Russians may not be as desperate to return to the European gas market as some might expect. It might demand some preconditions, such as the Europeans returning the massive central bank reserves that they froze. The EU used the proceeds from those funds to finance Ukraine, and the Russians might ask for those proceeds back as well.

Investment implications

Just in the past few days, the main European natural gas spot price started doing what I expected to see happen months ago, based on the unfolding fundamentals I described. The delayed market reaction arguably made the crisis worse, because higher market prices would have attracted more natural gas supplies over the past months, while now there is a higher gap, and a shorter timeframe for market forces to alleviate the situation. Fundamentals did not drive the market last year, but I think the market reached the point where it can no longer ignore those fundamentals. Low inventory levels are likely to keep European natural gas prices higher for at least the rest of the year, in order to avoid an even greater risk of physical shortfalls next winter than there is arguably toward the end of this winter.

Both Equinor and Cheniere are well-positioned to take advantage of this situation, and it is not entirely clear which one is more likely to outperform this year as a result of this all-important external factor. I am currently inclined with some degree of uncertainty to say that Cheniere will likely do better.

Equinor is Europe's largest natural gas provider. However, it is an integrated oil & gas company, with other business interests ranging from upstream, downstream oil interests, and windmills.

Cheniere, by contrast, is a company that is 100% exposed to LNG market trends, since it only produces LNG. If I am correct about the European natural gas market going higher this year, then Cheniere is in an obviously superior position to benefit relative to Equinor, since only part of Equinor's business will benefit from the same trend, versus 100% of Cheniere's business.

It should be noted, however, that Cheniere does have cost exposure to the surging US natural gas market price. It remains to be seen how the dynamics in the US market will play out in the next few weeks, as the expected demand surge from cooler weather plays out. It may be the case that US domestic natural gas prices will surge, in which case Cheniere can find that any LNG price gains will be wiped out by higher natural gas purchase prices.

The Trump administration may intervene out of political considerations by limiting LNG exports. Cheniere would then benefit from even higher European LNG prices, even as domestic natural gas prices would be kept in check. A prolonged cap on exports would potentially leave many of Cheniere's expansion projects in limbo if it is unable to expand LNG exports.

My Equinor position is currently significantly larger than my Cheniere position. I see it as being by far the safest option, with limited longer-term downside, since oil prices are currently at the minimum level needed to keep producers from dramatically pulling back on drilling. At the same time, the dividend, currently at 6% before Norway's special tax on foreign investors, makes this a good dividend investment, even if nothing else happens.

If my thesis of a sustained natural gas price rally in Europe turns out to be correct, there is arguably more upside for Cheniere, but if nothing much happens, Cheniere's dividend of 1% is not very enticing to keep investors interested in holding for a prolonged period, so there could be far more downside for this stock. LNG prices may surprise to the downside for many reasons. Cheniere also trades at a higher forward P/E ratio of over 12, compared with Equinor at 9, which arguably means that there is more potential downside.

Risks to my thesis

The idea that the Europeans could ever contemplate a pivot from framing Russia as their main threat to their very existence, to putting them in the position of saviors by resuming pipeline natural gas exports to the EU, may seem far-fetched. We do live in very unpredictable times; therefore, we should not entirely discount this possibility. Another severe energy crisis in Europe would be deeply damaging to the economy and to people's basic living standards; therefore, Europe's leadership may decide to act on a sudden rapprochement in the next few months. There is also always a chance that the weather will come to the rescue. The rest of the winter in Europe could turn out to be warm, sunny, and windy, creating the perfect conditions to cut natural gas demand.

Conclusions

At times, investors may get frustrated by a lack of market correlation to fundamentals. In this case, while at times I questioned my reasoning in the face of markets unexpectedly not reacting to ongoing fundamentals as expected, it turns out that the prolonged disconnect between fundamentals and Europe's natural gas markets was an opportunity to position myself for this moment. In the past week, it seems the markets finally gave up on ignoring the fundamentals.

Since I took up my initial position in Equinor stock in early February of last year, I made nine other additions to this stock in my portfolio, with my last purchase made in the middle of December. I also added Cheniere shares in two stages late last year, prompted by the selloff in its share price, even as the fundamentals were pointing to an ever more dire situation on the European gas markets. Based on recent European market moves, it looks like sticking with the fundamentals through last year, even as the market ignored them, is set to pay off this year, as the markets ran out of room to continue ignoring the facts.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Smurfit Westrock After Pricing News: Upside Looks Baked In
data.description:
Thesis

Smurfit Westrock Plc (SW) is a Dublin-based global leader in sustainable, paper-based packaging. The company's benefiting from renewed pricing power in containerboard as producers have cut about 10% of North American capacity, and Packaging Corporation of America’s (PKG) $70-per-ton price increase points to a tighter market. Even so, I’m staying neutral here. At this point, I think SW stock already prices in a lot of the good news.

The Setup for Pricing Power

PKG just announced a $70-per-ton increase effective March 1. After months of weak demand, too much inventory, and shrinking profit margins, it looks like the industry's trying once again to see how much it can raise prices. According to Philip Ng at Jefferies, it’s happening earlier than some people expected, but it lines up with the normal seasonal increase connected to agriculture and food shipments. More importantly, it’s happening at a time when a significant amount of capacity has already been taken out of the market.

Over the last year, containerboard producers have taken a sledgehammer to supply. About 10% of North American production capacity is now permanently gone, including roughly 3.9 million tons that were shut down just last year. It’s the most aggressive capacity reset the industry has seen in decades. International Paper (IP) and Georgia-Pacific (privately owned by Koch Industries) together shut down about 2.5 million tons of capacity last year. The impact's already showing up in the numbers. Per Morgan Stanley, even though demand's only growing at about 0.6%, production is being used much more fully, with operating rates nearing 93% overall and about 96% for kraftliner based on this year’s estimates.

For Smurfit Westrock, I think the timing here is hard to ignore. Coming off the merger, the company now has a much heavier North American tilt, with the region expected to account for roughly 64% of projected 2026 EBITDA. And this is exactly where pricing discipline is finally starting to return. Each dollar of higher pricing more directly turns into EBITDA, especially once volumes level out. We’re already seeing some early signs of that operating leverage. Last quarter, Smurfit put up $810 million of adjusted EBITDA in North America, which works out to a 17.2% margin. That’s an improvement versus earlier periods. And if prices hold and capacity utilization keeps tightening, then the earnings outlook heading into 2026 starts to look a lot more skewed to the upside.

That resilience isn’t totally accidental, but the result of a set of intentional and, I imagine, sometimes difficult decisions. Shutting down weaker facilities, like a corrugated plant in California, for example. And taking out about 500,000 tons of paper capacity across both containerboard and consumer grades. These improvements, along with a focus on value over volume that eliminated unprofitable business, put the company in a stronger position to benefit more from price increases than competitors that are less integrated.

With fewer assets and a more integrated way of operating, Smurfit is well positioned to benefit more than less integrated competitors if prices recover. Jefferies estimates that if the full $70-per-ton price increase is maintained across the industry, and assuming competitors like IP eventually follow, EBITDA could increase by 10–20%. For Smurfit Westrock, this could mean hundreds of millions of dollars in additional earnings, on top of the $400 million in run-rate synergies from the merger, which are already coming in higher than expected.

The amount of capacity they’ve taken out really changes the supply picture. And more often than not, it lasts longer than the market expects. Smurfit’s advantage in that environment comes down to scale and reach. They operate across EMEA, APAC, and Latin America. That's on top of North America, so the point is they can pull in global insights.

In Europe, where excess capacity is still a major issue, Smurfit’s mills operated at utilization levels in the mid-90% range last quarter and still achieved a 14.8% EBITDA margin, despite volatile input costs. Obviously, that speaks less to market conditions and more to operational control. Latin America also contributed, with margins above 21%, driven by export growth such as fruits and vegetables from Colombia, where volumes rose by 8%. I've highlighted these areas to show that having diversified sources of revenue helps protect the company when certain regions are weaker (overall though, the North American catalyst stands out).

Basically, the way it works is that by giving local managers responsibility for their own profit and loss, Smurfit cut the number of money-losing corrugated units in half within a year. This made 70% of its operations firmly profitable.

Looking out over the next 12–18 months, this pricing regime has the potential to carry Smurfit Westrock well into 2026 and beyond. Basically, if utilization stays where it is and demand picks up even a bit as the economy stabilizes, the company should be able to beat its $4.9–5.1 billion EBITDA guide for full-year 2025 without much strain.

Smurfit Westrock's Valuation

SW's at about a 17.6x blended P/E multiple on FAST Graphs. That sits between its long-term average of roughly 12.4x and higher forward estimates that already assume some upside. And a potential 10–20% EBITDA boost from higher containerboard pricing is already factored in margin expansion from the merger.

Earnings growth averaged a modest 3.65%. With pricing, merger synergies, and utilization all improving, earnings growth's now estimated at about 18.27%. Based on that outlook, fair value comes out to roughly $45.04 per share, hardly above the current share price.

Going into the February 11 earnings release, expectations are already high, with analysts forecasting 23.79% earnings-per-share growth in 2026. Weak demand could lead to more production downtime, increasing the risk of cash losses if fiber costs rise without any offsetting benefits, or even shareholder dilution if the company needs more debt and deleveraging stalls at 40.91% long-term debt-to-capital.

There's a bullish track (per FG above) in place for potential upside towards ~$74 if operating rates remain strong, although, competition could limit how far it goes. At an 18.06x forward non-GAAP P/E, I think we need to see the company beating expectations to justify that valuation.

Final Takeaway

Rating: Hold (with a bullish skew). The upside depends on pricing discipline staying in place and high utilization continuing through this year. Meanwhile, the downside risk comes from an already full valuation. Who should buy: Long-term, cycle-aware investors who are willing to be patient and believe containerboard pricing will hold and merger synergies will continue to exceed expectations. Who should avoid: Valuation-sensitive investors or short-term traders looking for quick gains from multiple expansion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rolls Royce's Set-Up For Robust Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (RYCEY) has recently seen robust growth in its share price, from a share price of Britich £5.9-V12.53 (a 112% upside) , attributed to recovery of long haul travels as well as high geopolitical tensions.

Despite the strong growth momentums in Rolls-Royce's recent share price, it is still relatively undervalued given the favorable long-term demand drivers.

I believe Rolls-Royce represents an valuable long-term investment opportunity and at its current price presents an attractive entry point, driven by skepticism in its sustainable top and bottom line growths.

Company Overview

Rolls-Royce was founded in 1904 and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom. It operates globally, with major revenue contributors including China, the US, the UK and throughout Europe.

Rolls-Royce is a designer, manufacturer and aftermarket service provider of engines, power generators, propulsion systems and others. It serves both the commercial and defense end-markets, operating in the defense, power systems, civil aerospace and SMR markets.

Robust Revenue Growth in Power Systems

According to McKinsey & Company, global data centre capacity demand is projected to growth at a CAGR of 22% from 2023 to 2030. Similarly, demand for advanced-AI capacity is expected to accelerate even faster, expanding at a CAGR of 33% over the same period.

As demand for data centre capacities increases, a surge in demand for power systems is required to keep these data centers up and running.

Goldman Sachs Research forecast data centre power demand to increase from 1-2% of global power demand in 2023 to 3-5% by 2030; in fact, demand for power is expected to exceed these forecasts as AI adoption has been accelerating faster than anticipated, driving up future forecasts.

Thus, the surge in power demand is creating a structural need for power systems, especially those with emergency backup capabilities. This is mainly because data centers must operate 24/7 with near-zero tolerance for downtime, so as to support continuous AI workloads and critical digital services.

Rolls-Royce is one of the three largest suppliers of emergency power system for data centers, holding 15% of global market shares for data centre backup installations. However, what I believe differentiates Rolls-Royce from competitors would be their mtu series' (emergency power system) ability to run on HVOs (biofuel). HVOs are produced from feedstock and produces much lower emissions relative to conventional fuels

This positions Rolls-Royce to capture market shares within the emergency power systems market as its mtu power systems are aligned with EU's climate objective to achieve net zero green house gas emissions by 2050. As an early mover in transitioning its power systems towards lower-emissions and alternative fuel solutions, I believe that Rolls-Royce is positioned to help customers navigate tightening environmental regulations, shifting away from less-adapted competitors and switching over to Rolls-Royce's power systems.

Overlooked Nuclear Energy Segment

Currently Rolls-Royce's SMR revenue segment accounts for less than 1 % of its total revenue, so it is expected that the markets would be more focused on its larger revenue generating core segments, namely civil aerospace, defense and power systems. This leads to little to no attention being paid to their SMR segment.

In fact, analysts consensus proves that it is being overlooked. Despite forecasted to see robust growths in revenue generated from SMRs, it is still minute relative to major revenue generating segments at billions of pounds.

I believe that the markets has yet to price in the long term upside embedded in Rolls-Royce's SMRs.

On September 2025, the UK and US signed an agreement to fast track nuclear energy development through streamlining regulations, sharing technical reviews and accelerating approvals.

UK governments is planning to strengthen commercial ties on nuclear power and increase nuclear energy that accounts for the region's power supply, from 14% to 25% by 2050, through bets across both large nuclear projects and SMRs.

Against this backdrop, Rolls-Royce was selected as the winner in the Great British Nuclear SMR competition, ahead of several international SMR vendors, essentially positioning them as the technological lead within SMRs market. Because of this, the UK government is backing Rolls-Royce in SMR productions through a £2.5 billion investment to provide 1.4GW of energy.

At the same time, Rolls-Royce has also partnered with CEZ, a.s. (CEZYY) on the development of 3GW of SMRs in Czechia. Using the UK government's investment as an estimate the development of 3GW of SMRs will roughly be worth an additional £5.36 billion in investments. Not to mention Rolls-Royce is also in talks with Swedish company Vattenfall for supplying SMRs as well as Turkey and other European countries, potentially generating even greater revenues than foreseen.

Defense Markets to Experience Greater than Forecasted Growths

During periods of high geopolitical tensions, defense stocks will increase in price. That is what Rolls-Royce is also experiencing as they serve defense markets through propulsion systems as well as engines for land, naval and air combat vehicles.

The defense markets is expecting to see structural tailwinds as NATO commits to higher defense spending (5% of their GDP) by 2035, creating a structural demand for defense procurements.

Looking at Rolls-Royce's revenue contributions, NATO accounts for 61.7% of its revenue, thus as political tensions escalates, it is supported by accelerated defense procurements driving Rolls-Royce to experience greater growths.

What made me believe Rolls-Royce's defense markets will see greater growths than expected was Rolls-Royce's CEO, Jörg Stratmann's, comments on recently receive an order of 300 mtu tank engines. What stood out to me was this specific line: ".... have recently significantly expanded our capacity for the development and production of urgently needed mtu drive systems for military vehicles ..."

This indicates to me that, Rolls-Royce's defense products is exceeding current production capacity, highlighting that growth momentum for their defence revenue will be much greater expected.

Revenue Forecast

To assess how exactly these revenue influencing factors will impact Rolls-Royce's share price, I used a DCF model and focused on forecasting future revenue segments.

For forecasting the revenue generated from SMRs, I estimated the total revenue generated using the UK government's £2.5 billion investment, supplying 1.4GW of energy. Using that, Rolls-Royce supplying GEZ 3 GW of energy will generate around another £5.36 billion in revenue, totaling to £ 7.86 billion. I then assumed revenue would be generated equally throughout the 5-year forecast, assuming Rolls-Royce does not finish production all at once.

NATO collectively spends around 2.71% of their GDP on defense. To ensure defense forecasts are it more conservative, I will assume the countries' GDP does not change, even though majority of NATO has seen growths in their GDP. Assuming NATO distribute their spending equally from 2025 to 2035 on defense, I grew the defense segment by 6.32% until the end of forecast year.

Goldman Sachs forecasts power demand to increase by 1.5x to 2x by 2030, hence I assumed a CAGR of 11%, dropping down to 5% as the market matures by 2029.

DCF

Tax rate is 25%, using the UK government's corporate tax rate.

Capital expenditure is expected to increase as Rolls-Royce increase their operational capacity to account for the increasing demand of their products. Therefore, I forecasted capital expenditure to grow in-line with revenue.

I assumed a more conservative approach, using the median terminal multiple at 22.32, ensuring forecasts are not too optimistic.

WACC was calculated using the UK's 10-year Gilt since Rolls-Royce is headquartered in the UK and a large portion of its revenue is derived from Europe.

I arrived at an implied share price of $14.65, a 14.38% upside from Rolls-Royce's current share price on its US ADR, proving that the company is still currently undervalued.

Risks and Concerns

The main risks regarding Rolls-Royce would be strict regulations SMR as it involves nuclear energy. This can result in prolonged approval timelines and delay the process of producing their SMRs. A potential consequence would be mid-process design changes mandated by regulators, which costs significant R&D and will result in less than desirable revenue outlook for Rolls-Royce's SMR segment.

However, I believe this is mitigated as the UK government is closely working with Rolls-Royce and regulators in different regions to expedite the production and export processes of its SMRs. Since the UK signed and agreement with the US, approval speeds and technical reviews of SMRs will significantly sped up to ensure that SMR projects are completed on time.

Conclusion

Overall I believe that Rolls-Royce is experiencing strong growth drivers across all end-markets it serves, underpinned by structural tailwinds such as increased defense spendings, accelerating demand for power systems and increased demand for nuclear energy within Europe.

Together these drivers supports sustained revenue growth and improves earnings, presenting an attractive risk-reward profile for long-term investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Aeluma: Separating Signal From Noise Amid An Unjustified Retreat
data.description:
Introduction

Author’s Note

During much of 2025 and early 2026, I have conducted an ongoing, in-depth diligence process on Aeluma (ALMU) to understand not only its technology but also its evolving commercial posture. My interest stems from how rarely investors encounter a public semiconductor company at this stage: one built on years of non-dilutive government funding, validated technology, and a growing roster of sophisticated customers—yet still early enough that the market has not fully adjusted its expectations.

As of January 27, 2026, Aeluma has retraced from $24 and change over two weeks to roughly $16 per share, with no new negative company-specific disclosures over that period.

Aeluma offers rare public‑market exposure to a monolithic III‑V‑on‑silicon photonics and optoelectronics platform with applicability across data‑center interconnect, sensing, quantum, and defense. The recent ~30% stock retracement appears driven by thin‑float dynamics, retail momentum unwind, and broader high‑beta de‑risking/Quant Hedge Fund short baskets — not by any disclosed deterioration in the underlying technical or commercial roadmap.

Investors should frame ALMU as a materials and device platform company whose value inflection will be driven by milestone progression (validation → qualification → pilot → design wins → revenue), not by short‑term price action or social‑media chatter.

At a sub‑$300M equity value, the market is effectively assigning little probability that Aeluma evolves into a meaningful photonics platform supplier. That mismatch between platform optionality and implied expectations is the source of the asymmetry.

$ALMU down ~30% in Two Weeks While Russell 2000 (IWM) Positive Over the Same Period

What Aeluma Actually Is (and Isn’t)

Aeluma is not a single‑product story.

It is a heterogeneous integration platform enabling direct growth of high‑performance III‑V compound semiconductors on large‑diameter silicon substrates. If successful at scale, this architecture potentially delivers:

  • Higher performance than silicon photonics alone
  • Lower cost and better scalability than traditional III‑V wafers
  • Tighter integration with CMOS manufacturing

This positions ALMU as a foundational enabler, not merely a component vendor.

Key application vectors include:

  • Optical interconnects for AI/data centers
  • Imaging, sensing, and LiDAR
  • Quantum photonics
  • Defense focal‑plane arrays

Optionality is a feature — but it also implies milestone‑driven timelines rather than linear product cycles.

In my prior articles, I described Aeluma as a “science project” in the public markets; today, it increasingly resembles a company preparing to execute on a defined commercialization path.

Platform First, Squeeze Second

The asymmetry in ALMU is not rooted in short interest or conference optics.

It is rooted in Aeluma’s position as an early‑stage III‑V‑on‑silicon materials and device platform. If the platform scales, value creation compounds across multiple product cycles rather than hinging on a single design win.

Short interest and thin float can amplify moves. They are accelerants, not the engine.

Where Real Signals Appear (and Where They Don’t)

Real early signals typically show up as:

  • External process collaborations
  • Packaging/integration partnerships
  • Reliability and lifetime testing milestones
  • Funded NRE engagements
  • Qualification builds
  • Pilot production

Real signals do not initially show up as:

  • Named Tier‑1 customers
  • Press‑released design wins
  • Revenue spikes

Those come later.

Investors should expect extended periods of quiet technical execution punctuated by discrete validation disclosures.

Recent technical-visibility events (such as Aeluma’s quantum-photonics presentation at SPIE Photonics West in January 2026) are examples of soft signals that support—but do not by themselves prove—commercial traction.

Commercialization Pathway

Typical sequence:

  1. Lab validation
  2. External partner replication
  3. Reliability testing
  4. Qualification builds
  5. Pilot production
  6. Design win
  7. Revenue ramp

ALMU today appears to be transitioning from pure R&D toward early commercialization and remains in the front half of this curve.

The most important future disclosures will relate to steps 3–5.

Interpreting Recent Stock Action

The two-week move from roughly $24 → $16 occurred without any disclosed negative fundamental development.

This type of drawdown is consistent with:

  • Thin‑float microcap structure
  • Momentum unwind
  • Short‑term trader exit
  • Basket‑level quant short positioning across unprofitable growth names

It is not evidence of thesis impairment.

For long‑horizon investors, price weakness without thesis impairment generally improves risk/reward.

Against this backdrop, ALMU increasingly resembles a live asymmetric setup into tangible catalysts rather than a deteriorating fundamental story. The stock’s decline appears technical and flow-driven, with no new negative company disclosures, no derailed milestones, and no adverse news since the recent run, even as broader high-beta sentiment has softened.

Soft Signals vs. Hard Signals

Some investors track LinkedIn followers, likes, and general industry visibility.

These are soft corroboration signals at best:

  • They may indicate technical relevance
  • They may indicate ecosystem awareness

They do not confirm:

  • Partnerships
  • NDAs
  • Commercial agreements
  • Design wins

Hard validation will come through milestone disclosures.

Government / Defense Pathway

Defense and government programs often represent:

  • Early commercial‑grade qualification environments
  • Smaller volumes
  • High performance requirements
  • Long validation cycles

Follow‑on awards, scope expansions, and multi‑year program continuity matter more than headline dollar size.

Historically, defense traction frequently precedes commercial adoption in advanced semiconductor platforms.

Capital Structure & Runway

Key variables to monitor:

  • Quarterly burn rate
  • Cash runway
  • Ability to fund qualification internally
  • Non‑dilutive funding (grants, government programs, NRE)

Avoiding premature heavy dilution materially increases long‑term upside.

Broader High‑Beta Photonics / Defense Dislocation

The early weeks of 2026 have challenged high‑growth cyclicals, with rotation toward defensives amid AI “overbuild” and efficiency debates. Company‑specific triggers — offerings, insider sales, and filing headlines — are exaggerating moves, especially in thinly traded names.

Across optical, defense, and space verticals, multi‑year investment cycles tied to AI‑capable data centers, national security hardening, and advanced sensing remain intact.

Below is a consolidated snapshot of several fundamentally sound but currently dislocated names often discussed alongside ALMU. A recent post from Crux Capital highlighted a similar group of high-beta optics, defense, and space-exposed companies; the table below reflects my interpretation and positioning.

Source: Crux Capital on X (Crux Capital Post)

High‑Beta Optics / Defense / Space Basket

Ticker

Post’s Thesis

My Take

$AAOI

Optical transceivers as an AI networking bottleneck; operating leverage on volume inflection

Structural demand for high‑speed optics endures; near-term watch hyperscaler capex commentary — if AI spend holds, this can snap back quickly

$ALMU

III‑V on silicon for photonics / SWIR sensing cost unlock; early‑stage leverage point

Intriguing photonics platform with potential cost and integration advantages; recent pressure reflects AI “overbuild” and efficiency fears more than company‑specific issues; if silicon integration keeps advancing, 2026–2027 adoption could re‑rate the name meaningfully

$ASPI

Nuclear fuel cycle leverage; exposure to enrichment scarcity

Macro / energy‑security story with indirect AI tailwinds via datacenter power demand; volatility is high, but structural drivers remain in place

$ASTI

Space‑based power tech; speculative but defensibly themed

High‑beta with real execution and funding risk; upside tied to converting concept and defense adjacency into contracted programs

$KOPN

Optics supplier for AR/defense; embedded in programs

DoD‑linked programs provide some cushion; less “AI headline” exposure but reasonable hold through turbulence

$LASR

High‑power directed energy: moving toward procurement phase

Defense‑budget momentum and transition from R&D to procurement are underappreciated; easing rates could support multiple expansion

$LASE

Industrial lasers with defense/manufacturing crossover

Hit hard by sector rotation and filing noise, sensitive to manufacturing cycles, but recovery in capex could offer leverage

$LPTH

Vertically integrated IR optics supplier; supply‑chain re‑shoring advantage

Benefiting from U.S.‑aligned sourcing and restrictions on materials like germanium, well-positioned if an AI‑driven photonics “supercycle” materializes

$OPXS

Polymer‑based optics for defense / autonomous systems

Niche but growing exposure to lightweight optical components; low liquidity keeps volatility elevated

$POET

Photonics packaging for AI interconnects

Recently hit by a large offering and dilution concerns, but the underlying silicon‑photonics packaging thesis remains intact if execution improves

$RDW

Space infrastructure and national‑security platform

Aligned with rising global space and natsec budgets; high beta but durable multi‑year support

$RKLB

Vertically integrated launch provider

Sentiment pressured by insider sales/offerings more than fundamentals; liquid proxy for broader space enthusiasm

How to Frame ALMU as an Investment

ALMU should be viewed as:

  • Early‑stage platform technology
  • High volatility
  • High uncertainty
  • Potentially very high asymmetry

This is not primarily a trading vehicle.

It is a milestone‑driven option on successful heterogeneous photonics integration.

Position sizing should reflect that reality.

Bottom Line

Short‑term price action is noise.

The thesis hinges on:

  • Technical validation
  • External replication
  • Qualification milestones
  • Gradual ecosystem integration

Until those break, volatility does not equal failure.

For investors willing to look out 12–36 months, periods of non‑thesis‑driven weakness often represent better entries, not worse ones.

Author’s Note: This article was written entirely by the author without the use of generative AI tools. All analyses and conclusions are original and based on publicly available information.

data.title: SPYI Vs. QQQI: Why I Prefer The SP500-Based Covered Fund
data.description:
QQQI and SPYI: current volatility is too low

I last covered Neos S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (BATS:SPYI) on Nov 26, 2025, with an article titled “SPYI Vs. SPY: The Case For Covered Calls In Two Charts”. The article rated SPYI as a buy rating after assessing its valuation and dividend yield against the SP500 index as approximate by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). As for the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (NASDAQ:QQQI), I last covered it on July 18, 2025, with an article titled “QQQI ETF: I See Better Hiding Places Amid Record NASDAQ Levels”. The article rated QQQI as a hold after considering the NASDAQ index’s valuations at that time and QQQI’s partial option overlay.

Since those writings, a few parameters of relevance to covered call options have changed materially and motivated this follow-up. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top two changes as I see: the changes in the volatilities and also the concentration risks of their underlying assets. These changes have led me to maintain an overall positive outlook for covered call funds like SPYI and QQQI as a hedge strategy under current market conditions. But I will explain a few factors that make SPYI the superior choice for this purpose than QQQI, mainly the high concentration and valuation risks underlying the Nasdaq relative to the SP500 index.

Let me start with the first factor. The chart below shows the 30-day rolling volatility for the SPY and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) ETFs, as proxies for the indices underlying SPYI and QQQI funds, respectively. As seen, both QQQ and SPY’s volatilities have muted significantly since my last writings. Currently, QQQ’s volatility is only 14.44% and SPY’s reading is 10.72%. Both are significantly below their 52-week averages (of 21.02% and 15.62%, respectively) and hovering near their 52-week lows. The subsequent sections will detail my thoughts on such muted volatility on QQQI and SPYI’s return potentials.

SPYI and QQQI: basic information

To better understand the relevance of volatility, an introduction to the basic facts and investment strategy of SPYI and QQQI is in order. Both funds actively use data-driven call option strategies to generate income, maximum upside capture, and minimize tax headwinds. More details of their strategies are quoted below from their fund facts. Both funds are popular choice in the space of covered call funds with sizable and similar AUM as seen in the table below. Both funds charge an expense ratio of 0.68% and both are classified as nontraditional equity fund due to their option overlay.

QQQI fund descriptions: The Fund seeks to distribute high monthly income generated from investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq-100® Index and implementing a data-driven call option strategy. Actively managed by NEOS, the Fund seeks to take advantage of tax loss harvesting opportunities in addition to utilizing NDX Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower 60/40 tax rates. The Fund utilizes a call option strategy that may include both sold and purchased NDX index options, which may provide the opportunity for upside capture in rising equity markets.

SPYI fund description: SPYI aims for tax-efficient and high monthly income by actively investing in stocks and options on the S&P 500 Index. The fund employs a call spread approach that uses SPX index option futures contracts. SPYI holds the individual stocks of the S&P 500 Index (SPX) combined with a call spread strategy. The fund seeks to generate high monthly income through dividends from stocks and premiums from SPX call options.

As a reflection of their investment strategies, the next two charts display and also compare their top holdings. As seen, many of their top holdings overlap. Both funds have substantial exposure to tech mage-caps such as Nivida (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), etc. However, their specific weights differ quite a bit. Overall, SPYI is more diversified and less top heavy than QQQI. For example, its exposure to is “only” 7.8% (vs. 8.8% for QQQI) for NVDA and is 6.3% (vs. 7.1% for QQQI) for AAPL.

Next, I will explain the implications of such differences.

SPYI is better prepared for a volatility surge

To continue the discussion of volatility, in addition to the unusually low realized volatility, the implied volatilities (IV) for both SPY and QQQ are also quite low in the option market as you can see from the following 2 charts. SPY’s IV is only 12.8 currently. This puts it in the bottom 25% percentile rank historically (i.e., the IV was lower in the current level only 25% of the time last year). QQQ’s IV is 17.5 currently, putting it in the bottom 35% percentile rank in the past year.

The combination of such low IV and high valuation metrics for both SPY and QQQ signal investor complacency, and such complacency underestimate the true risks in my view given the many macroscopic risks undergoing. As such, I consider covered-call options timely.

Between QQQI and SPYI, I like SPYI better due to its better diversification and reduced exposure to mega-tech stocks such as the mag 7. Overall, QQQI’s top 10 assets make up 47.97% of the fund, compared to a more balanced 38.34% for SPYI. With QQQI’s higher concentration risks, when coupled with the unusually low volatility and the Nasdaq’s elevated valuation risks, I see a better return/risk ratio in SPYI.

Other risks and final thoughts

Of course, QQQI is a solid choice too for the reasons mentioned above (and I am rating it as hold, not sell). To offset its concentration risks and valuation risks relative to SPYI, QQQI offers noticeably higher dividend yield as shown in the chart below. QQQI’s current yield of 13.84 percent is higher than SPYI’s distribution rate of 11.73 percent by more than 18 percent in relative terms, thus offering a thicker downside cushion. Although note that the current yields of both funds are above their four-year averages, signaling attractive valuation for both funds.

Before closing, a couple of other risks are worth mentioning. If you recall from an earlier chart, SPYI maintains a highly efficient 2.00% turnover rate, in contrast to QQQI’s 22.00% turnover rate. A higher turnover rate typically generates more costs, trading frictions, and also tax headwinds. There are also some downside risks that are generic to covered-call funds compared to passively indexed equity funds. The use of covered options limits the upside price potential in a market rally, and this limitation applies to both SPYI and QQQI. Also, the active management style also leads to higher expense ratios for these funds (0.68% for QQQI and SPYI as mentioned earlier) which far exceed the fees charged by large passive equity ETFs (in the range of 0.05% these days for many of them these days).

To close, this comparison of SPYI and QQQI is motivated by the changes of a few parameters of relevance to covered call options. In particular, I believe the volatilities underlying both SPY and QQQ have become too low to be sustainable with the ongoing geopolitical risks. The combination of unusually low level of volatility and high valuation risks make the consideration of option overlay timely in my experience. Between SPYI and QQQI, I see an even better reward/ratio in SPYI given its more diversified holdings and lower valuation risks relative to the Nasdaq index.

data.title: USA Rare Earth: It's Hard To Bet Against The Government (Upgrade)
data.description:
USA Rare Earth, Inc. (USAR) has been on a wild ride over the past year. The former SPAC nearly doubled in March 2025 before subsequently slumping below the initial $10 offering price.

Then, in October, the stock soared to as high as $44/share. But just a couple of months later, the stock slumped back into the teens. Now, though, the stock has doubled once again to start 2026:

I last covered USAR stock in October, saying that shares were a sell at $35. While its recent moves were interesting, there wasn't enough fundamental support to justify that lofty price. That seems to have been a solid call, albeit with the stock moving back up sharply from the recent lows.

In any case, after all this volatility and now, with the transformational deal that the company announced over the weekend, where does that leave USAR stock today? While I still have my concerns, the combination of the government's involvement along with the lower share price is enough for me to upgrade to a Hold rating. Here's why.

The Federal Government Takes a Stake in USA Rare Earth

US Rare Earth stock surged on Monday following news that the U.S. government came to an agreement to take a major investment stake in the company.

From the presentation slide deck, here's the key one highlighting the deal terms and what the funding is intended to achieve in coming years:

I saw some folks on social media describing this as a $1.6 billion investment in the company, which is true in one sense. However, it's important to note that most of the funding comes in the form of a $1.3 senior secured loan at a reasonably low-interest rate. Meanwhile, there is a more modest $277 million of direct equity funding via the issuance of 16.1 million USAR shares at $17.17 each.

The equity component can get larger as well, as the government is also getting a 17.6 million share block of warrants with an equivalent $17.17 exercise price. If the government ultimately does convert those warrants into stock, that would roughly double the size of the cash injection into the company. Regardless, the majority of the headline figure here is through the loan facility rather than stock and warrants.

It's worth noting that the equity offering is going to end up being fairly dilutive as well, especially if the warrants are exercised as well. The $17.17 issuance point isn't terrible; after all, the SPAC deal went off at $10/share, and the government is paying a decent premium to that. However, USAR stock was trading up in the $30s not long ago, and now the government is getting a fat block of stock at $17. And for anyone buying USAR stock in the mid-$20s now on the news, keep in mind you're paying a roughly 50% premium to where the government just put in its own funds.

Enough about the fundraising. What does the influx of new money mean for business development? Here is a slide laying out the new roadmap:

Thanks to the transaction with the U.S. government, USA Rare Earth now has secured funding for its planned mining & processing. It is also roughly doubling its planned magnet making while dramatically increasing its anticipated metal making capacity.

This is still going to be a slow-moving story. As the tables above show, the company is anticipating minimal production across these three lines of business in 2026 and only modest production in 2027 before things start to kick into gear in 2028. So, for the next couple of years, USAR stock is likely to remain highly volatile and be driven by broader narratives and themes as investors wait for proof as to how well the operating business ultimately performs.

While uncertainty remains high, however, the balance sheet is now in great condition, and that greatly limits intermediate-term downside. At a minimum, I'd expect the stock to have strong technical support at $17/share, as that's what the government paid for its equity stake in the company.

Broader Context Around the Deal

While the Biden Administration did provide some funding and incentives to support the rare earths industry, these efforts have dramatically intensified under the Trump Administration. Meanwhile, the current administration's tariff threats and the trade dispute with China in particular have brought rare earths into the spotlight. Specifically, China has signaled that it would be willing to use rare earth exports as a pressure point in trade disputes.

This combination of increased government support for U.S. rare earths producers along with the threat of Chinese supply being cut off has created an ideal environment for stoking investor interest in rare earths stocks. However, will this favorable macroeconomic backdrop persist once President Trump's time in office concludes? It's also worth noting that there are midterm elections coming up in November, and betting markets currently suggest the Democrats are favorites to win back control of the House.

I offer that context when considering this statement from USA Rare Earth's deal-related conference call, which it held on Monday. CFO William Steele noted that:

"Yes. So, both the $277 million direct funding award and the $1.3 billion loan are intended to be disbursed based upon milestones that naturally result from our business plan. And those will be disbursed for the most part between '26, '27 and '28. There's a little bit of a tail piece going into '29."

I read that to mean that there is still operational risk here. USA Rare Earth seemingly needs to hit certain business development milestones to get all the headline funding figures. This is a fine way to organize the contract, and assuming the company can deliver on its projected timelines, it should be able to obtain this funding. But, to be clear, this isn't just a huge cash infusion up front; the company needs to hit some clear metrics to get the full funding package.

USA Rare Earth CEO Barbara Humpton further supported that view with this comment:

"[A]s we achieve milestones, taxpayer support continues to build, but the American taxpayer is never overcommitted on this plan. They're never the ones leading and out in front."

One other point to consider is that potentially, if political winds shift in Washington and budget priorities change, it would be worth revisiting how concrete those later payments would be. I think the market may be underestimating how much volatility we could see in certain energy and mineral stocks if the Supreme Court rules against the legality of the tariffs or the Democrats end up regaining control of Congress this fall. There's been a lot of money flooding into things such as nuclear power and rare earths, driven by excitement around the Trump Administration's budgetary priorities, but that sentiment could easily flip if Democrats gain a stronger footing in Washington.

The point of this discussion isn't to knock the deal. This is indeed a big win for USA Rare Earth in terms of providing both funding and credibility. And in particular, the U.S. government's involvement helps differentiate USA Rare Earth from the increasingly crowded pack of other companies that are competing within the rare earths space.

However, if you were surprised at why the stock sold back off so sharply after the initial pop, I think these factors explain the market's less enthusiastic reaction as opposed to, for example, when MP Materials (MP) shares skyrocketed following its respective deal with the government back in 2025.

Market Reaction

While the deal is clearly a positive for USA Rare Earth, the market reaction ended up being a lot less upbeat than many traders were probably expecting.

Indeed, from a pre-market high of $37/share early Monday morning, USAR stock ended up giving back fully $10/share of gains by the end of the day. While USAR stock closed up 8% for the day, shares actually rallied less than they did on Friday, the prior trading day before the deal was announced. And shares are trading a bit lower once again in Tuesday's action as of this writing.

Interestingly, a variety of other popular stocks in the niche metals space fell sharply on Monday following the USA Rare Earth news:

This is a stark contrast to back in 2025 when MP Materials got its deal with the U.S. government. That announcement led to a major spike for a variety of its other peer stocks in the rare earths space. But with this deal, the peers had a sell-the-news reaction, and USAR stock gave up a big chunk of its gains as well.

A fundamental explanation for this could be that investors are starting to price more competition into the rare earths space over the long term.

Furthermore, there's no guarantee that there will be permanent demand for U.S.-sourced rare earths. Getting back to my earlier point, much of the excitement around the industry is due to the Trump Administration's escalation of trade tensions with China.

If a future administration takes a different stance on Chinese trade and their rare earths export market can freely compete with U.S. producers, will a company like USA Rare Earth have a solidly profitable product? We've seen predecessor rare earth companies, such as Molycorp, for example, go bust in the past due to cheaper international competition. Given that USA Rare Earth isn't generating meaningful revenues yet and most of the potential value here comes from years far out into the future, it's hard to assign a high confidence level to projected future cash flows given how quickly the tariff and trade situation keeps on changing.

USAR Stock Verdict

I've been bearish on USA Rare Earth because I believed that the market was way ahead of itself in terms of valuing the company. The firm's processing and refining capacity is still commercially unproven as yet. I have my questions over the value of the U.K. business that they acquired. Meanwhile, on the mining side, I think it's informative that their Round Top deposit has been around for decades without any successful attempts at turning it into a working mine; it's not like this is some hot new exploration find on the rare earths front.

That said, it's awfully hard to bet against USAR stock outright at this point with the direct governmental backing. The firm now has a strong balance sheet and clearly has enough cash to ramp up to production. Management still has a ton to prove. But the valuation is no longer as demanding as before, and they have added a credible backer in the form of the U.S. government. That's enough to equalize the risk/reward profile. So, I'm upgrading shares to a Hold.

data.title: Honda: Turning Bullish On Positive Developments (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've raised Honda Motor Co., Ltd.'s (HMC) rating from a "Hold" to a "Buy."

I'm confident about HMC's future performance in the American market. Chip-related headwinds are a thing of the past. The group is also seeking ways to optimize EV production expenses.

Also, it's now better positioned to ride on the "Software Defined Vehicle/SDV" wave by increasing its stake in Astemo to above 60%.

The firm's mixed 2QFY2026 (YE March) showing was the subject of my prior Nov. 10, 2025 write-up.

JV Capital Injection Supports Growth Acceleration

As per a mid-Jan SA News article, HMC has guided for U.S. sales of "1.5M vehicles in 2026." This implies that volume expansion could go from a flattish 2025 to the current year's "+4%."

I'm inferring from the above-mentioned outlook that its supply chain challenges should have been resolved.

At the 2QFY26 call (S&P Capital IQ transcript) last year, HMC projected "a volume reduction of 110,000 units (Apr '25-Mar '26) in North America" resulting from "semiconductor shortages."

One of its suppliers was previously the primary cause of disrupted production.

My November 2025 update highlighted that it "single-sourced certain chips from Nexperia." But a recent Jan. 14, 2026 Associated Press piece indicated that the latter's "export ban was later lifted."

Earlier, HMC disclosed at the end-2025 that it will "purchase the U.S. (Ohio) battery manufacturing assets of its joint venture partner, LG Energy Solution (LGES)" for almost $3 billion.

I think this cash infusion will enhance the HMC-LGES JV's profitability by repaying debt and cutting interest expenses. That should translate into cheaper batteries for the partners (including Honda) in future.

HMC's Jan 14 media release stated that its "electrified models" had registered "record sales for the third straight year." It also noted that the "Acura RSX electrified SUV," slated for a 2H26 launch, will "be the first Acura EV model produced at the Marysville Auto Plant in Ohio."

It's likely the company might have abandoned specific EV plans and lost share to rivals in the past. Elevated battery costs meant that some projects weren't viable. There was also limited flexibility for price discounting to gain customers.

In my opinion, a financially stronger LGES-HMC JV enables a shift towards EVs, boosting HMC's chances of meeting the '26 target of 1.5 million.

Gaining Majority Control Of Key Supplier Is Beneficial

Last month, HMC issued a 6-K filing revealing "an additional 21% equity interest in Astemo" to convert it into "a consolidated (61%-owned) subsidiary."

The near-term financial impact is favorable based on my assessment.

Astemo's EBIT in the most recent fiscal year was ¥67 billion, according to S&P Capital IQ data. I estimate that the former's inclusion in the group boosts HMC's operating income by +6% on a pro forma basis.

Also, the firm's financial improvement prospects have gotten better by penetrating the SDV space with its investee.

An Overview Of The New Daughter Company's SDV-Related Offerings

Bernstein published a research note (not publicly available) titled "Mobility Revolution" on July 11, 2025. The brokerage forecasted that the worldwide "SDV sales penetration" will rise from 8% for the current year to 53% at the end of this decade.

Going forward, HMC's integration of Astemo's capabilities should give it an edge in the fast-growing SDV market. The latter's "Internet of Vehicles/IOV" Platform" enables remote wireless upgrades. A 1Q25 poll indicated that over three-quarters of auto market participants are anticipating a "broader deployment" of "over-the-air (OTA) updates" in the next two years.
